title: Osmotic lysis = cell wall synthesis inhibitor (cefuroxime)
vignette: Gram-positive bacteria are injected under the skin of experimental animals. Three days later, the animals are treated with antibiotic therapy. Aspirates taken from the infection site are plated on nutrient-rich agar. The isolated bacteria assume a spherical configuration when placed in an isotonic solution and disintegrate rapidly when placed in a hypotonic solution. Which of the following antibiotics was most likely administered to the animals in this experiment?

clues.note: Gram-positive organisms have a thick peptidoglycan cell wall that provides rigid structure and protects against osmotic lysis. This makes the wall the relevant target.

clues.text: spherical configuration when placed in an isotonic solution
clues.note: Losing the rigid peptidoglycan wall makes a bacterium collapse into a sphere (protoplast/spheroplast). In isotonic fluid it survives because osmotic forces are balanced - but its shape is no longer maintained.

clues.text: disintegrate rapidly when placed in a hypotonic solution
clues.note: Without a wall, water rushes in down the osmotic gradient and the cell LYSES in hypotonic solution. Osmotic fragility = the cell wall was disrupted, so the drug given was a cell-wall-synthesis inhibitor.

whatMatters: Spherical shape in isotonic fluid + lysis in hypotonic fluid = the rigid peptidoglycan wall was destroyed. That points to a cell-wall-synthesis inhibitor. Among the options, only cefuroxime (a cephalosporin/beta-lactam) blocks cell wall synthesis. Answer B.
strategy: Translate the osmotic clue: shape lost + lyses when water enters = no functional cell wall = a cell-wall-synthesis inhibitor. Then scan the options for the beta-lactam/glycopeptide. Cell-wall inhibitors = penicillins, cephalosporins (cefuroxime), carbapenems, monobactams, vancomycin, fosfomycin. Everything else here is a protein-synthesis or DNA inhibitor and wouldn't change osmotic stability.

eliminations.label: Azithromycin - 50S protein synthesis inhibitor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a macrolide that binds the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocks protein synthesis. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it does not affect the peptidoglycan wall, so bacteria would keep their shape and osmotic stability. ✅ Correct if: asked for a 50S-binding protein synthesis inhibitor.

eliminations.label: Chloramphenicol - 50S protein synthesis inhibitor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: binds the 50S subunit (peptidyl transferase) to inhibit protein synthesis; can cause dose-related myelosuppression and aplastic anemia. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: no effect on the cell wall -> no osmotic fragility. ✅ Correct if: a 50S inhibitor with marrow toxicity is sought.

eliminations.label: Ciprofloxacin - DNA gyrase/topoisomerase inhibitor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a fluoroquinolone that inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, causing DNA damage and death. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it kills by DNA disruption, not wall disruption, so bacterial shape/osmotic stability is unchanged. ✅ Correct if: a DNA gyrase inhibitor is the target.

eliminations.label: Doxycycline - 30S protein synthesis inhibitor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a tetracycline that binds the 30S subunit to block aminoacyl-tRNA entry. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it inhibits protein synthesis, not peptidoglycan synthesis - no effect on the wall's rigidity. ✅ Correct if: a 30S-binding agent is sought.

reasoning: Spherical in isotonic + lysis in hypotonic = loss of the rigid peptidoglycan wall
Loss of wall integrity implicates a cell-wall-synthesis inhibitor
Among the choices, only cefuroxime (cephalosporin/beta-lactam) blocks cell wall synthesis
mechanism: The bacterial cell wall is rigid, cross-linked peptidoglycan that gives the organism its shape and protects it from osmotic lysis in changing tonicities. When peptidoglycan synthesis is inhibited, the bacterium can no longer maintain its shape (collapsing into a sphere) and loses osmotic protection, so it survives in isotonic solution but bursts in hypotonic solution as water floods in. Beta-lactams such as cefuroxime bind penicillin-binding proteins and block the transpeptidation cross-linking step of wall synthesis; other wall-active drugs include penicillins, carbapenems, monobactams, vancomycin, and fosfomycin. The remaining options inhibit protein synthesis (azithromycin, chloramphenicol, doxycycline) or DNA (ciprofloxacin) and therefore would not produce osmotic fragility.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro/Pharm > antibiotic mechanisms: cell wall synthesis inhibitors (penicillins, cephalosporins [cefuroxime], carbapenems, monobactams, vancomycin, fosfomycin) vs protein synthesis (30S: tetracyclines/aminoglycosides; 50S: macrolides/chloramphenicol/clindamycin/linezolid) vs DNA (fluoroquinolones).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Decode the osmotic experiment: lysis in hypotonic solution = no functional wall = a cell-wall inhibitor was used. Then pick the beta-lactam.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on peptidoglycan, protoplast/spheroplast, isotonic vs hypotonic, osmotic lysis, transpeptidation, or penicillin-binding protein? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The protein-synthesis options (azithromycin, chloramphenicol, doxycycline) are common antibiotics, but none touch the wall - only cefuroxime causes osmotic fragility.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: osmotic fragility/lysis = cell wall inhibitor (beta-lactam/vancomycin/fosfomycin); cefuroxime = 2nd-gen cephalosporin; 30S = tetracyclines/aminoglycosides, 50S = macrolides/chloramphenicol.

keywords.front: Bacteria lyse in hypotonic solution after antibiotic

keywords.back: Penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems, monobactams, vancomycin, fosfomycin

firstAid.detail: Pharm > beta-lactams (cephalosporins, e.g., cefuroxime) bind PBPs, block transpeptidation; osmotic lysis when wall lost.
firstAid.location: Pharm > beta-lactams (cephalosporins, e.g., cefuroxime) bind PBPs, block transpeptidation; osmotic lysis when wall lost.

firstAid.detail: Micro > rigid wall prevents lysis; protoplasts/spheroplasts lyse in hypotonic media.
firstAid.location: Micro > rigid wall prevents lysis; protoplasts/spheroplasts lyse in hypotonic media.

memoryHook: No wall, no ball-shape, no protection: a bug that pops in water lost its peptidoglycan - so the drug was a wall-builder blocker (cefuroxime).

stem.note: Gram-positive organisms have a thick peptidoglycan cell wall that provides rigid structure and protects against osmotic lysis. This makes the wall the relevant target.
